title: द्वितीयोनुवाकः
samhita: नमो॑ हिरण्यबाहवे सेना॒न्ये॑ दि॒शा-ञ्च॒ पत॑ये॒ नमो॒
pada: नमो॑ । हिर॑ण्य-बाहवे । से॒ना॒न्ये᳚ । दि॒शाम् । च॒ । पत॑ये । नमो॒ ॥
krama: नमो॑ हिरण्यबाहवे । हिर॑ण्यबाहवे सेनान्ये᳚ । से॒ना॒न्ये दि॒शाम् । दि॒शा-ञ्च॑ । च पत॑ये । पत॑ये॒ नमो॒ । नमो॒ इति॑ नमो॒ ॥

sayana.text:
Bhatta-Bhaskara-Bhashya: For this anuvaka starting with 'Namo hiranyabahave', the Rishi is Manduka. The Metre is Maha-Gayatri. Rudra is the Deity. There, having pacified the Lord Rudra and his means of destruction through the first anuvaka, and having had them withdrawn, he now begins to praise and salute him in his universal form (vaishvarupya) through the next eight anuvakas. Regarding that, Shakapuni says: "In three anuvakas (2nd, 3rd, 4th), a single Yajus starts with 'Namah' and ends with 'Namah'." Yaska says: "The 'Namah' at the start is one Yajus, and the 'Namah' at the end is another Yajus." Kashakritsna says: "The eight anuvakas are eight (long) Yajus mantras." There, praise and salutation are for pairs of Rudras. As seen in the Shatapatha Brahmana: "One offers to two and two." Even in a single entity, the duality is established by imposing a difference based on eight different causes of salutation; where many are mentioned, one should be understood as an adjective for the others. For example: "Salutation to the golden-armed commander of armies, and to the lord of the directions." Among them, some have salutations at both ends (ubhayato-namaskara), and some have salutation at only one end. There is a Brahmana text on this: "Those Rudras which have salutations at both ends are more fierce (ghoratara). And those which have salutation at only one end are more peaceful (shantatara)." Formerly, when prayed to by Brahma for the sake of creation, the God created Rudras of similar nature to himself. It is said elsewhere: "Equal in creation, power, and wisdom." There, salutations are made to those attendants of the God (in plural forms); or in the singular forms, salutations are made everywhere to everything out of the suspicion that the God resides there.

Now we state the meters of the individual Yajus: for those of one syllable, Daivi Gayatri; two syllables, Daivi Ushnik; three syllables, Daivi Anushtup; four syllables, Daivi Brihati; five syllables, Daivi Pankti; six syllables, Daivi Trishtup or Yajushi Gayatri; seven syllables, Daivi Jagati or Yajushi Ushnik; eight syllables, Prajapatya Gayatri or Yajushi Anushtup; nine syllables, Yajushi Brihati or Asuri Jagati; ten syllables, Yajushi Pankti or Asuri Trishtup; eleven syllables, Yajushi Trishtup or Asuri Pankti; twelve syllables, Yajushi Jagati or Prajapatya Ushnik or Asuri Brihati. Or, the Gayatri of the Samas, etc., should be understood according to the scriptures. There, at the beginning, to show that "this universe which is yours, you are its Master, and no Master harms his own property," he establishes the God's Lordship over the universe (vishva-patitva). And because of its primacy, first the material...
...

abhinava_shankara.text:
Second Anuvāka

    "Salutation to the golden-armed one (Hiraṇyabāhave), to the leader of armies (Senānye), and to the lord of the directions (diśāṃ ca pataye namaḥ)."

Thus in the First Anuvāka, the Blessed Lord along with his retinue was propitiated through fifteen mantras. Now, in the Second and subsequent eight anuvākas, the Blessed Lord is saluted in his form as the Lord of all (sarveśvara) and as the indwelling self of all (sarvāntaryāmin). Beginning here [with this mantra] and extending through "abhayaḥ apatisyaśca vo namaḥ," all mantras have the structure of salutations (namaskāra) both at the beginning and end. In each mantra, "namas" appears at the beginning and "namaḥ" at the end, making each a single complete mantra — for example: "namo hiraṇyabāhave senānye diśāṃ ca pataye namaḥ." The same holds for those that follow. Now then, in order to establish the universal lordship (sarveśvartva) of the Lord by first demonstrating his sovereignty over individual things within the manifest world, the commentary begins: "Namo hiraṇyabāhave iti" — Hiraṇyabāhave: to the one whose arms are adorned with golden armlets (keyūra) and the like. The mention of arms (bāhu) is also indicative [of the whole body]: from head to foot his entire divine auspicious form is of a golden hue, endowed with supremely celestial golden ornaments — thus do the Vedic texts proclaim. For example: "Namo hiraṇyabāhave hiraṇyavarṇāya" (salutation to the golden-armed, golden-coloured one), "Ābhraṇakhāt sarve suvarṇaiḥ" (all [his limbs] are golden from ornament to skin), "Yadāpamayaḥ paśyate rukmavarṇam" (when the sage perceives him of golden colour) — and so forth. The Smṛti texts too: "Rukmārsaṃ svapravīgamyam" (perceivable by one of purified mind), "Cārucāmīkarābhāsam" (resplendent like beautiful gold) — and so forth.

Senānye: the senānī is one who leads armies (senā nayati — the one who leads the host). He is the lord of the various armies in the form of the aggregations of all divine beings (devādinā) assembled to combat the sufferings of the world. In the Āśvalāyana school, introducing Śiva, it is stated: "All consecrations (nāmadheyāni) indeed belong to him; all armies (senāḥ) and all standards (ucchrāyaṇāni)." Thus, as senānī, and also as the lord of the directions (diśāṃ — from east onwards, pataye — to the lord), may there be salutation. [The grammatical rule] "ṣaṣṭhīyuktaścha chandasi vā" accounts for the ghi-designation (a special grammatical form). Here the primary syntactic connection is "diśāṃ pataye namaḥ." Now, how is the Supreme Lord the lord of the directions, when Indra, Agni and others are lords of the directions? To dispel this doubt, "Senānye" has been specified — through which it is shown that it is the Supreme Lord, as the overlord of all the hosts of divine beings, who has lordship over the directions; Indra and the others are merely lords of their respective directions by his command. And regarding the question of how the Supreme Lord can be lord over Indra and the others, "Hiraṇyabāhu" is specified — by which the form of the Supreme Lord that is worshipped in the Antara-Āditya vidyā (the meditative practice of [the Lord] within the sun) is meant, as [the Vedic text states]: "He who is that golden-hued person within the sun (ya eṣo'ntarāditye hiraṇmayaḥ puruṣaḥ)." Hence the application of "namo hiraṇyabāhave" in the Antara-Āditya vidyā is made in the Purāṇas and other texts. "Those worlds above, and whatever there are of the worlds [below] — he is the lord of those, and of whatever they desire for themselves" — thus the Antara-Āditya vidyā —
abhinava_shankara.sanskrit:
द्वितीयोऽनुवाक: ॥

    नमो हिरण्यबाहवे सेनान्ये दिशां च
पतये नम: ॥

   तदेवं प्रथमेऽनुवाके पञ्चदशिभिर्मन्त्रैर्भगवान् सपरिकर:
प्रसाध प्राथित: । अथेदानीं द्वितीयादैरष्टभिरनुवाकै: सर्वे-
श्रत्वसर्वान्तर्यामिभितवरूपेण भगवन्तं नमस्किरण्यन् प्रथमं
सर्वेश्वरत्वेन नमस्करोति । एतदारभ्य 'अभय: अप-
तिष्यश्च वो नम:' इत्यन्तं सर्वे एव उभयतो नमस्कारा पत्वे
मन्त्रा: । सर्वत्र च नमआदिर्नमोऽन्त एकैको मन्त्र:, यथा
— 'नमो हिरण्यबाहवे सेनान्ये दिशां च पतये नम:'
इति । प्रवमरेऽपि । तत्र तावत् प्रपञ्चान्तर्गतैकैकवस्त्वा-
धिपत्यप्रदर्शनपूर्वकं भगवत: सर्वेश्वरत्वसुपलपाद्यन् नमस्क-
र्तुमारभते— नमो हिरण्यबाहवे इति । हिरण्यबाहवे हि-
रण्यकेयूराद्यलंकृतबाहवे, बाहुमहणं चोपलक्षणम्, आपाद-
मस्तकमतिदिव्यकनकखचितभूषणतया स्वरूपेण च सर्वोऽपि
कनकवर्ण एव दिव्यमङ्गलविग्रह इति हि श्रुत्य: प्रथयन्ति ।
यथा— 'नमो हिरण्यबाहवे हिरण्यवर्णाय' 'आभ्रणखात्सर्वे
